questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe the structure of an atom and nucleus.

learningObjective.statement: Describe the nuclear model of the atom. (Atomic Structure)

questions.skill: Describe the nuclear model of the atom.
questions.stem: For atomic structure, which statement correctly shows how to describe the nuclear model of the atom? For atomic structure, choose the statement that fits this exact GCSE Physics idea.
questions.question: For atomic structure, which statement correctly shows how to describe the nuclear model of the atom? For atomic structure, choose the statement that fits this exact GCSE Physics idea.
options: Incorrect recall: the atom is a solid sphere with no internal structure. This is not valid for atomic structure.
Misremembered idea: most of the atom is filled with positive charge.
The atom has a tiny positive nucleus containing protons and neutrons, with electrons outside it.
Wrong definition: electrons are found inside the nucleus with neutrons. That would miss the atomic structure focus.
questions.correctAnswer: The atom has a tiny positive nucleus containing protons and neutrons, with electrons outside it.
questions.explanation: 6.1 targets: Describe the nuclear model of the atom. Focus area: Atomic Structure. For this question, The atom has a tiny positive nucleus containing protons and neutrons, with electrons outside it. Context used: a required-practical notebook; response mode: direct_recall. The rejected options each confuse this with a different misconception rather than the named specification point.

distractorRationales.misconception: The atom is a solid sphere with no internal structure.

distractorRationales.misconception: Electrons are found inside the nucleus with neutrons.

This reads the pattern as a positive ion forms by gaining extra electrons.

questions.correctAnswer: Use this idea: A positive ion forms when an atom loses one or more outer electrons.

distractorRationales.text: The data would not show that a positive ion forms by gaining neutrons. This is not valid for atomic structure.

distractorRationales.text: This ignores the evidence: a positive ion forms when protons leave the nucleus. That would miss the atomic structure focus.
distractorRationales.misconception: A positive ion forms when protons leave the nucleus.

distractorRationales.text: This reads the pattern as a positive ion forms by gaining extra electrons.
distractorRationales.misconception: A positive ion forms by gaining extra electrons.

options: Incorrect correction: medical radiation is useful because it is never ionising. This is not valid for radiation safety.
Keep the misconception: sterilising equipment works by making it permanently radioactive.

Ionising radiation can sterilise equipment or treat cancer when the dose and exposure are controlled. That is the valid GCSE Physics conclusion.

questions.question: A small data set about radiation safety is used to test whether students can choose a suitable half-life for a use. Which interpretation is valid? Focus on the medium demand: choose a suitable half-life for a use.
options: Use this idea: A useful isotope must have a half-life long enough to work but short enough to limit unnecessary dose.

This reads the pattern as the safest isotope is always the one with the highest activity forever.

questions.question: A student is comparing similar ideas in internal energy. Which statement should they keep to describe internal energy? Focus on the medium demand: describe internal energy.

learningObjective.statement: Explain energy during change of state. (Internal Energy)

questions.skill: Explain energy during change of state.
questions.stem: A small data set about internal energy is used to test whether students can explain energy during change of state. Which interpretation is valid? Check the wording against the internal energy clue before choosing.
questions.question: A small data set about internal energy is used to test whether students can explain energy during change of state. Which interpretation is valid? Check the wording against the internal energy clue before choosing.
options: Use this idea: During a change of state, energy changes particle potential energy while temperature stays constant.
This ignores the evidence: energy is not transferred during boiling. That would miss the internal energy focus.
The data would not show that temperature always rises during melting. This is not valid for internal energy.
This reads the pattern as particles are destroyed during a change of state.
questions.correctAnswer: Use this idea: During a change of state, energy changes particle potential energy while temperature stays constant.

Context used: a required-practical notebook; response mode: data_interpretation. The rejected options each confuse this with a different misconception rather than the named specification point.

distractorRationales.text: The data would not show that temperature always rises during melting. This is not valid for internal energy.
distractorRationales.misconception: Temperature always rises during melting.

distractorRationales.text: This ignores the evidence: energy is not transferred during boiling. That would miss the internal energy focus.
distractorRationales.misconception: Energy is not transferred during boiling.

distractorRationales.text: This reads the pattern as particles are destroyed during a change of state.
distractorRationales.misconception: Particles are destroyed during a change of state.
